narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as an uphill battle—89% likelihood of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. At the top of what stood out was your first-year decile of 1.0, which signaled to the model a shaky academic start right out of the gate, the strongest pull toward struggle that it couldn't ignore. Your LSAT score of 37 followed close behind, reinforcing that early impression by suggesting challenges with the kind of high-stakes reasoning the bar demands. Then there was your third-year decile, also a 1.0, painting a picture of grades that stayed in the bottom rung throughout school, making it hard for the model to see upward momentum. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, oddly aligned in the model's view with profiles that faced bar hurdles, perhaps because it didn't offset other gaps as strongly as hoped. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 added to that narrative, hinting at consistent but not standout performance under pressure. As a white male going full-time, those details layered on subtly, matching historical patterns where similar students hit roadblocks more often. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's this combination that whispered a story of potential mismatch with the bar's rigor, leading us to protect our program's outcomes. You've got real strengths elsewhere, like targeting law with clear intent, and this isn't the end of your story; many rebound by addressing early grades head-on. Consider targeted prep or a gap year to build that academic decile higher—it could rewrite your next chapter. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong.
